questions.text: What are the bakers known as in Goa?
questions.explanation: The traditional village bakers in Goa are still called 'pader'.

questions.text: What was the characteristic sound of the baker's arrival?
options: The thud and jingle of a bamboo staff
questions.explanation: The baker made a musical entry with the 'jhang, jhang' sound of his specially made bamboo staff.

questions.text: The traditional dress worn by the Goan baker was known as:
questions.explanation: The baker or pader of those days wore a peculiar dress known as the 'kabai', a single-piece long frock reaching down to the knees.

questions.text: How many times a day did the baker visit the house?
questions.explanation: He came twice: once in the morning on his selling round, and then again after emptying his huge basket.

questions.text: Where were the monthly accounts of the baker recorded?
options: On some wall with a pencil
questions.explanation: Baking was a profitable profession, and accounts were traditionally marked on walls.

questions.text: A marriage gift in Goa was considered meaningless without which sweet bread?
questions.explanation: Just as a party or a feast loses its charm without bread, a marriage gift is incomplete without the 'bol'.

questions.text: What was the main attraction for the children when the baker arrived?
questions.explanation: While the loaves were for the elders, children specifically looked for the bread-bangles, sometimes carefully chosen sweet bread.

questions.text: Which of these describes the physical appearance of a baker?
questions.explanation: Bakers were traditionally prosperous, and their plump bodies were testimony to their well-being.

questions.text: Who brought the loaves of bread from the baker's basket?
questions.explanation: The children were often pushed aside so the servants could collect the loaves.

questions.text: Baking was considered a ______ profession in the old days in Goa.
questions.explanation: The baker and his family never starved; it was a lucrative trade.

questions.text: Coorg is situated between which two places?
questions.explanation: Coorg, or Kodagu, is the smallest district of Karnataka, located midway between Mysore and the coastal town of Mangalore.

questions.text: The people of Coorg are possibly of ______ or ______ descent.
questions.explanation: One story suggests they are descendants of Alexander's army (Greek), while their dress suggests Arabic origins.

questions.text: The long black coat with an embroidered waist belt worn by the Kodavus is called:
questions.explanation: Known as 'kuppia', it resembles the 'kuffia' worn by Arabs and Kurds.

questions.text: Who was the first Chief of the Indian Army?
questions.explanation: General Cariappa, the first Chief of the Indian Army, was a Coorgi.

questions.text: The river ______ obtains its water from the hills and forests of Coorg.
questions.explanation: Kaveri is the main river of the region, rich in freshwater fish.

questions.text: Coorg is known for which of these plantations?
questions.explanation: Coorg is famous for its coffee estates and evergreen rainforests.

questions.text: Which large freshwater fish is found in the Kaveri river in Coorg?
questions.explanation: Mahaseer is a large freshwater fish that abounds in these waters.

questions.text: The people of Coorg are the only people in India permitted to:
questions.explanation: Kodavus are known for their bravery and are exempt from the Indian Arms Act.

questions.text: What is the best time to visit Coorg?
questions.explanation: The season of joy commences from September and continues till March.

questions.text: From the top of which hills can one see a panoramic view of the entire misty landscape of Coorg?
questions.explanation: A walk to the Brahmagiri hills brings you into a panoramic view of Coorg.

questions.text: Which island can be reached by crossing a rope bridge in Coorg?
questions.explanation: Crossing the rope bridge leads to the sixty-four-acre island of Nisargadhama.

questions.text: Where is India's largest Tibetan settlement located in Coorg?
questions.explanation: The Buddhist monks in red, ochre, and yellow robes are found at Bylakuppe.

questions.text: Coorg is the ______ district of Karnataka.
questions.explanation: It is the smallest district in the state of Karnataka.

questions.text: The climb to the Brahmagiri hills leads to which type of view?
questions.explanation: The summit provides a 360-degree 'panoramic' view of the misty landscape.

questions.text: Which birds are common in Coorg?
questions.explanation: Kingfishers dive for Mahaseer in the river waters.

questions.text: In the story 'Tea from Assam', where were Rajvir and Pranjol going?
questions.explanation: Pranjol had invited Rajvir to visit his home in Assam during the summer vacation.

questions.text: How many cups of tea are drunk daily throughout the world?
questions.explanation: Rajvir shared this impressive fact with Pranjol during their train journey.

questions.text: What was Pranjol's father's profession?
questions.explanation: Pranjol’s father was the manager of Dhekiabari, a tea garden in Upper Assam.

questions.text: According to the Chinese legend, why did the emperor always boil water before drinking it?
questions.explanation: The legend says he boiled water for safety, and one day tea leaves fell into it.

questions.text: Who was Bodhidharma?
questions.explanation: Bodhidharma was an Indian legend who discovered tea plants growing from his eyelids.

questions.text: Why did Bodhidharma cut off his eyelids?
questions.explanation: To avoid falling asleep during meditation, he cut off his eyelids.

questions.text: The words 'chai' and 'chini' are derived from which language?
questions.explanation: These words have their origin in China, where tea was first drunk.

questions.text: Tea was first drunk in China as far back as:
questions.explanation: History records tea drinking in China since 2700 BC.

questions.text: When did tea come to Europe?
questions.explanation: Tea reached Europe in the 16th century, primarily as a medicine.

questions.text: Assam has the largest ______ of tea gardens in the world.
questions.explanation: Assam is famous for having the highest density of tea plantations globally.

questions.text: What is the name of the tea estate managed by Pranjol’s father?
questions.explanation: The train stopped at Mariani junction, from where they drove to Dhekiabari Tea Estate.

questions.text: Which 'sprouting period' yields the best tea in Assam?
questions.explanation: The second-flush or sprouting period from May to July produces the finest tea.

questions.text: How did the tea pluckers look, as described by Rajvir?
questions.explanation: Among the orderly rows of bushes, the tea pluckers moved like dolls.

questions.text: Which town is mentioned as the starting point of Rajvir and Pranjol's train journey?
questions.explanation: The boys were studying in Delhi and took the train from there to Assam.

questions.text: What is the tone of the chapter 'Glimpses of India'?
questions.explanation: The chapter provides a rich, informative account of different regions and cultures of India.
